name: Monaco

outbound_official.notes: Monaco's official government portal (monservicepublic.gouv.mc) does NOT publish a comprehensive outbound visa-free country list for Monaco passport holders. The 'Formalities for stays abroad' page directs Monegasques to contact the consulate or embassy of each destination country individually to determine visa requirements. The only specific destinations mentioned are: (1) France — no visa required for Monegasque nationals to study, work, or settle; (2) United States — covered by the US Visa Waiver Program (VWP), 90-day maximum stay; (3) United Kingdom — ETA required from 2 April 2025 (£10, valid 2 years) for stays under 6 months. No aggregated origin-side country list was found on any Monaco government domain (gouv.mc, maef.gouv.mc). Rely on destination-side data for a full access matrix.

visa_free.nationality: EEA nationals and countries grouped as 'associated states' by Monaco (including UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City, Switzerland)
visa_free.notes: Monaco's official embassy and monservicepublic.gouv.mc pages list the following countries whose nationals need only a valid passport or identity card for stays under 3 months: Germany, Andorra, Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Cyprus, Denmark, Spain, Estonia, Vatican City (Holy See), France, Finland, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Italy, Iceland, Latvia, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Norway, the Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Czech Republic, Romania, United Kingdom, San Marino, Slovakia, Slovenia, Sweden, and Switzerland. This includes true EEA members (EU + Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway), Switzerland, and four non-EEA European states (UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City) that Monaco treats equivalently.

Nationals of countries that are exempt from the Schengen short-stay visa requirement (for stays under 90 days in any 180-day period) may enter Monaco without a separate visa. Monaco has no independent visa system; it follows Schengen external border rules via bilateral agreements with France (1997). The official Monaco pages direct visitors to France's Service-Public.fr for the authoritative list of exempt nationalities. UK nationals are confirmed Schengen-exempt post-Brexit (up to 90 days in any 180-day period). Note: ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) is expected to launch in the last quarter of 2026 and will add a pre-travel authorisation requirement for these visa-exempt nationalities transiting through Schengen territory to reach Monaco.

visa_policy.notes: Monaco does not issue its own entry visas. The Principality is within the Schengen external border framework by virtue of bilateral agreements with France (1997). EEA nationals and those Monaco groups as 'associated states' (including UK, Andorra, San Marino, Vatican City, Switzerland) need only a passport or ID card for stays under 3 months. Non-EEA nationals who are not Schengen-visa-exempt must hold a valid Schengen short-stay visa obtained from the Schengen country serving as their European entry point — once obtained, no additional formalities are required for Monaco. Monegasque residence permits allow non-Schengen nationals to travel to, from, and within the Schengen area without a short-stay visa. ETIAS, expected in late 2026, will require pre-travel authorisation for all Schengen-visa-exempt non-EEA nationals (including those transiting Schengen to reach Monaco).

cbi.notes: Monaco has no citizenship-by-investment program. Monegasque nationality can be acquired by: (1) filiation (paternal or maternal / biological or adoptive); (2) declaration after 20 years of marriage to a Monegasque national (reduced to 10 years for those married before 1 July 2022), requiring continued cohabitation and not having previously acquired Monegasque nationality by marriage; (3) naturalisation, requiring at least 10 years of ordinary residence in Monaco from age 18, renunciation of prior nationality, and exemption from military service in country of origin — granted solely at the discretion of H.S.H. the Sovereign Prince, who may dispense with the residency requirement. Dual citizenship rules differ by acquisition route: naturalised citizens must renounce prior nationality; spouses acquiring by declaration may retain dual nationality if their foreign law permits. Source: https://monservicepublic.gouv.mc/en/themes/nationality-and-residency/monegasque-nationality/acquisition-and-loss-of-nationality/acquiring-monegasque-nationality

programs.name: Monaco Residence Permit (Carte de Séjour)
programs.notes: Any person aged 16 or over wishing to stay in Monaco for more than 3 months must obtain a residence permit. The official page specifies no fixed minimum financial amount — it requires demonstration of sufficient funds via salary, professional income, or bank reference from a Monaco bank confirming sufficient funds ('the sum which is judged sufficient depends on the banking establishment in Monaco providing the reference'). The widely cited €500,000 figure appears only in law-firm/aggregator sources, not on gouv.mc. Non-EEA nationals must first obtain a long-stay Type D visa from French authorities (or transfer from French residency of over one year). The first permit fee is €80. The 'Privilege' permit (10 years residency) is the precondition for naturalisation eligibility. A valid Monegasque residence permit allows non-Schengen nationals to travel within the Schengen Area without a short-stay visa.

agreements: Schengen Area (de facto, via bilateral agreements with France — 1997 exchange of letters adapting the 1963 Convention on Good Neighbourly Relations; Monaco's Heliport and Port of Condamine recognised as authorised external border crossing points by Schengen Executive Committee decision of 23 June 1998)
Customs Union with France (Monaco within EU customs territory)

validation_notes: CHANGED: First visa_free entry expanded to enumerate the exact 34 countries/entities listed on Monaco's official embassy pages (previously described generically as 'EEA nationals and associated states'). The official list explicitly includes UK, Andorra, San Marino, and Vatican City — none of which are EEA members but which Monaco treats equivalently. The prior record's 32-country count was imprecise.
CHANGED: Second visa_free entry (Schengen-exempt non-EEA nationals) updated to note ETIAS launch expected in last quarter of 2026, which will add a pre-travel authorisation step for these nationals.

CHANGED: cbi.dual_citizenship_allowed changed from false to null. The prior record stated 'dual citizenship is not permitted' but re-verification shows this is route-dependent: naturalised citizens must renounce prior nationality, but spouses acquiring nationality by declaration MAY retain dual nationality if their foreign law permits. The blanket 'false' was inaccurate.

CHANGED: cbi.notes updated to accurately reflect dual citizenship rules by acquisition route and to note the Sovereign Prince's discretionary power to dispense with the residency requirement for naturalisation.

CHANGED: rbi entry updated to clarify that the residence permit fee of €80 is for the first permit issuance, and added that a valid Monegasque residence permit allows non-Schengen nationals to travel within the Schengen Area without a short-stay visa (confirmed from monservicepublic.gouv.mc/residence-permit-and-travel).
CHANGED: official_domains expanded to include embassy-to-uk.gouv.mc, ambassade-en-australie.gouv.mc, and ambassade-en-inde.gouv.mc which were checked.

UNCHANGED: conditional_access remains empty. No official Monaco source grants visa-free or conditional-access entry on the basis of holding a non-Schengen credential (US visa, UK ILR, Canadian PR, etc.). The Schengen visa mechanism is standard, not a conditional-access rule. No OCI provision exists.

UNCHANGED: No diplomatic/service passport bilateral visa-suppression agreements could be confirmed from official Monaco sources (gouv.mc, legimonaco.mc, journaldemonaco.gouv.mc). Aggregator claims remain unverifiable.

ADDED: outbound_official field added. Monaco's official government portal does not publish a comprehensive outbound visa-free country list for Monaco passport holders. The 'Formalities for stays abroad' page on monservicepublic.gouv.mc directs citizens to contact destination-country embassies individually. Only France (no visa), USA (VWP), and UK (ETA from April 2025) are specifically mentioned.
gaps: Specific enumerated list of all Schengen visa-exempt third-country nationalities not published on Monaco's own official pages — Monaco's official sites defer to France's Service-Public.fr for this list.

The official Monaco residence permit page does not state a fixed minimum bank deposit amount — it states the amount 'depends on the banking establishment.' The widely cited €500,000 figure appears only in law-firm/aggregator sources, not on gouv.mc, and has not been verified.
No fast-track, digital nomad, startup, or talent-visa scheme found on official Monaco sources.

Diplomatic/service passport visa waivers: Aggregator sources list countries whose diplomatic/service passport holders are reportedly exempt from Monaco visa requirements (Algeria, Angola, Bahrain, Bolivia, Indonesia, Kuwait, Morocco, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Tunisia, Turkey, United States). No published bilateral visa-suppression agreements for diplomatic/service passports were found on official Monaco treaty repositories (legimonaco.mc, gouv.mc, journaldemonaco.gouv.mc). Cannot be recorded as verified.

ETIAS launch date: Expected 'last quarter of 2026' per official search results, but the precise launch date has not been officially confirmed. Once ETIAS launches, visa-exempt non-EEA nationals transiting Schengen to reach Monaco will need ETIAS authorisation.
The en.gouv.mc EU relations page timed out and could not be fully verified.

outbound_official: Monaco government publishes no comprehensive outbound visa-free list. Only France, USA (VWP), and UK (ETA) are explicitly mentioned on official pages. Full country access matrix must be derived from destination-side data.
